segments.paradiso-31-g32: Certo is an adverb ('certainly'). 'tra esso e ’l gaudio' is a prepositional phrase meaning 'between it and the joy' (esso = demonstrative 'it'; ’l = the). 'mi facea' uses the imperfect tense of fare (facea, archaic spelling of faceva) with the clitic mi as indirect object ('it made/caused me'); libito is a noun meaning 'inclination/desire' that functions as the effect caused by facea. The clause 'non udire e starmi muto' lists two infinitival complements: udire (not to hear) and starmi (the infinitive stare with enclitic -mi, 'to remain for me' = 'to remain/keep silent'), with non negating the first infinitive and understood to apply to the second as well.
segments.paradiso-31-g33: E quasi = 'and as if'. peregrin is an archaic form of pellegrino ('pilgrim'). 'che si ricrea' is a relative clause: che = who; si ricrea uses the reflexive verb ricrearsi meaning 'refreshes himself'. 'nel tempio del suo voto' is a locative phrase 'in the temple of his vow'. The final riguardando is a present participle/gerund ('looking back' or 'looking at') that modifies the action of the pilgrim.
segments.paradiso-31-g34: e spera già = 'and already hopes' (spera is present indicative of sperare; già = already). ridir is an infinitive 'to say again' that takes the subordinate clause com' ello stea as its content: com' = come 'how'; ello = 'he/it'; stea is an archaic/poetic form of stare (here subjunctive/imperf. sense) rendering 'he was/stood' in the dependent clause describing what the speaker hopes to repeat.
segments.paradiso-31-g35: su per = 'up through' (su + per indicating upward movement through something). 'la viva luce' = 'the living/vivid light' (viva = vivid or living, modifying luce). passeggiando is a gerund 'walking/strolling', indicating the manner in which the subject is moving (it links to the following main clause: while walking up through the light...).
segments.paradiso-31-g36: menava ïo = imperfect tense 'I was leading' or 'I led' (menava = archaic imperfect of menare; ïo = io 'I' with dieresis). 'li occhi' is the direct object 'the eyes'. 'per li gradi' = 'along the steps/degrees' (per indicating motion along), so the phrase means 'I let my eyes travel along the steps'.
segments.paradiso-31-g37: mo sù = 'now up'. mo is a frequent medieval/poetic particle meaning 'now' and sù is the adverb 'up'. This begins a series of small adverbial phrases describing the motion of the eyes.
segments.paradiso-31-g38: mo giù e mo recirculando = 'now down and now recircling/going round again.' giù = 'down'; e = 'and'; recirculando is a gerund/participle 'circling again' or 'going round again', describing the repeated motion. The construction mo...mo indicates alternation ('now... now...').
segments.paradiso-31-g39: Vedëa is the first‑person imperfect/past form (archaic spelling) meaning “I saw” or “I beheld.” The phrase's noun phrase follows: visi (faces) is the direct object; a + carità here means “by/through charity” (agency or motivating cause). Süadi is a poetic participle/adjective modifying visi; its precise lexical value is irregular/archaic and must be read as “moved/urged” in context.
segments.paradiso-31-g40: d’ (di) introduces the agent/source: “by/with/from.” Altrui is a possessive adjective meaning “another’s/others’,” modifying lume (“light”). Fregiati is a past participle used adjectivally: “adorned.” The coordination e di suo riso links a second adornment introduced by di (again marking source/means) + suo riso (“her/its smile”).
segments.paradiso-31-g41: e introduces another coordinated element: atti (acts, gestures) is the noun; ornati is a past participle adjective meaning “adorned.” Di introduces the quality/ornamentation: tutte onestadi = “all kinds of honesty/honourable qualities.” The phrase describes the manner/virtue that decorates those acts.
segments.paradiso-31-g42: La forma general di paradiso is a noun phrase “the general form of Paradise.” Già tutta mïo sguardo avea compresa uses an order where the possessor phrase (mïo sguardo) rather than the subject is prominent; avea compresa is the pluperfect/imperfect perfect construction (avea + past participle) meaning “had encompassed/embraced.” Compresa functions as the past participle describing what the gaze had taken in.
segments.paradiso-31-g43: in nulla parte ancor fermato fiso is an elliptical locative clause meaning “not fixed on any part yet.” In + nulla parte = “on no part/nowhere”; ancor (ancora) = “still/not yet.” Fermato (past participle of fermare) + fiso (archaic/felt form of fisso) together mean “fixed” or “stopped,” describing that the gaze was not fixed on any single place.
segments.paradiso-31-g44: e volgeami con voglia rïaccesa introduces a change of attention: volgeami is volgere + clitic mi (I turned myself / I turned), imperfect tense used narratively. Con voglia rïaccesa is an instrumental phrase “with a desire rekindled.” Per domandar is purpose (“in order to ask”), and la mia donna di cose di che la mente mia era sospesa is an object clause: “my lady about those things concerning which my mind was suspended,” where di che introduces the content about which the mind was held in suspense.
segments.paradiso-31-g53: Ond' is a contracted, poetic form meaning 'thereupon' or 'therefore' and introduces direct speech; elli is an archaic nominative pronoun ('he') naming the speaker while the verb 'said' is omitted (understood) after the phrase.
segments.paradiso-31-g54: The infinitive phrase 'A terminar lo tuo disiro' = 'in order to end your desire' (A = 'in order to' + infinitive); mosse is a past-tense (perfect) form from muovere used here as 'moved/caused' with Beatrice as subject and me as direct object (Beatrice moved me). 'del loco mio' means 'from my place' (del = di + il, here 'from the').
segments.paradiso-31-g55: 'e se riguardi sù' = 'and if you look up': e = 'and'; se = conditional 'if' introducing a clause; riguardi is 2nd-person singular present ('you look' / 'you turn your gaze'); sù = 'up' or 'above'. 'nel terzo giro dal sommo grado' specifies location: 'in the third circle (giro) counting from the highest degree/step' (dal = from the, sommo grado = highest degree/step).
segments.paradiso-31-g56: Main future clause: 'tu la rivedrai' = 'you will see her again' (rivedrai = future, 2nd sing.). 'nel trono' = 'on the throne'; the relative clause 'che suoi merti le sortiro' = 'which (that) her merits will assign to her': che = relative 'that/which'; suoi merti = 'her merits' (possessive before noun); le is the indirect-object pronoun 'to her'; sortiro is an archaic future form meaning 'will assign / will result in (for her)'. Word order places the possessor phrase before the verb and the clitic le before the verb, as in older/poetic Italian.
segments.paradiso-31-g57: Sanza risponder is archaic wording for modern senza rispondere: “without answering.” Sanza (= senza) governs the infinitive/gerund that follows and creates an adverbial clause of manner.
segments.paradiso-31-g58: li occhi sù levai places the object (li occhi = the eyes) before the verb for emphasis; levai is the first‑person past tense (levare, to raise) meaning “I raised” or “I lifted.” Sù is the adverb “up.” The construction is simple SOV inversion typical of poetry.
segments.paradiso-31-g59: e vidi lei che si facea corona is “and I saw her who was making/forming a crown/halo”: vidi is the perfect past “I saw,” che introduces the relative clause, si + facea (imperfect of fare) is reflexive/passive‑like “was making for herself,” and corona here means halo/ring. Riflettendo da sé li etterni rai is a gerundial phrase (“reflecting the eternal rays from herself”) describing the means by which she formed the halo; da sé indicates the rays originate from her.
segments.paradiso-31-g60: Da introduces the source or point of departure: 'From that region'. che introduces a relative clause modifying 'regïon' (regïon che più sù tona = 'the region which sounds highest above'). più sù tona is a poetic expression: più (more) + sù (up/above) with tona (from tonare, here 'resounds'/'is sounded'), so the clause means 'which resounds higher up'. The second half of the line places the subject 'occhio mortale' (mortal eye) before the verb dista (present indicative) in an inverted order typical of poetry: 'no mortal eye is so distant.'
segments.paradiso-31-g61: qualunque begins a comparative parenthesis meaning 'whichever/any that...'; in full it implies 'whichever (eye) in the sea abandons itself lower'. The reflexive s’ (si) with abbandona makes the verb middle/reflective: 'abandons itself' or 'gives itself up' (here 'sinks' / 'plunges'). The prepositional in mare più giù gives the location 'into the sea, farther down'.
segments.paradiso-31-g62: quanto introduces the comparative 'as much as' linking the preceding parenthesis to the main clause; lì da Beatrice = 'there from Beatrice' (lì 'there' locates Beatrice) and la mia vista is the possessive + noun 'my sight'. Word order is poetic: 'as much as my sight was from Beatrice there.'
segments.paradiso-31-g63: ma nulla mi facea is an elliptical clause meaning 'but it did nothing to me' or 'it affected me not'. nulla is the negative pronoun 'nothing', mi is the indirect object 'to me', and facea is the imperfect of fare (here meaning 'to cause' or 'to affect'), so the imperfect tense expresses an ongoing state in the past.
segments.paradiso-31-g64: ché = 'for/because' introduces the causal clause. süa is an archaic spelling of sua ('her'), and effige is the noun 'effigy/likeness' (Dante's orthography). discendëa is an imperfect of discendere 'to descend' (with diaeresis indicating vowel break), here meaning 'did not come down' or 'was not transmitted to me'. per mezzo mista = 'by/through a mixed medium' (mezzo = 'means' and mista 'mixed'), explaining why the sight produced no effect.
segments.paradiso-31-g65: O is the vocative particle 'O/Oh' addressing Beatrice; donna is the direct address 'lady'. in cui is the relative 'in whom' (cui = whom/which), la mia speranza vige uses vige (present indicative of vigere) meaning 'lives/prevails/is in force'—a present-state verb describing where Dante's hope 'resides'.
segments.paradiso-31-g66: e che soffristi is a relative clause 'and who suffered/underwent' (soffristi is 2nd person singular past of soffrire, here addressing Beatrice). per la mia salute 'for my salvation' gives the motive/reason, and in inferno lasciar le tue vestige is an infinitive phrase 'to leave your traces in Hell' (lasciar as infinitive, vestige = traces/footprints); the order is typical Dantean syntax with infinitive placed after the location.
segments.paradiso-31-g67: di tante cose quant’ i’ ho vedute is a comparative/partitive phrase: 'from so many things as I have seen.' quant' (short for quanto) + i' (here representing the subject 'io', 'I') + ho vedute (present perfect 'I have seen') — vedute functions as a past participle/adjectival noun 'seen'/'that I have seen'.
segments.paradiso-31-g68: dal tuo podere e da la tua bontate is the source: 'from your power and from your goodness.' riconosco is first-person present 'I recognize/acknowledge', and la grazia e la virtute are the direct objects 'the grace and the virtue' (virtute here meaning moral excellence). The two 'da' phrases indicate causes or sources for Dante's recognition.
segments.paradiso-31-g109: Io is the emphatic subject pronoun 'I'. Levai is the poetical passato remoto (simple past) first-person singular of levare, meaning 'I raised' or 'I lifted'; li occhi is the direct object (li = archaic plural masculine article). Word order is subject–verb–object and the semicolon closes the clause.
segments.paradiso-31-g110: This whole clause is a simile introduced by "e come" ('and as'). "Da mattina" ('at morning') gives the temporal setting for the general observation. "La parte orïental de l’orizzonte" is a noun phrase ('the eastern part of the horizon') where "de" is the archaic form of di. Soverchia is present indicative 3rd sing. of soverchiare used as a timeless/general present 'outshines/surpasses', and the relative clause "quella dove 'l sol declina" ('that which/part where the sun declines/sets') identifies the contrasted part.
segments.paradiso-31-g111: Così is a discourse adverb ('thus/so') that links the preceding simile to what follows; it signals the result or parallel that Dante is about to draw.
segments.paradiso-31-g112: Quasi introduces a figurative comparison ('as if'). The phrase "di valle andando a monte" contains the gerund andando (from andare) meaning 'going' and expresses the movement of the eyes metaphorically — 'as if (they were) going from the valley up to the mountain'. "Con li occhi" ('with the eyes') is an instrumental phrase specifying that the motion is of Dante's gaze. Word order (gerund + prepositional phrases) is typical of poetic compression.
segments.paradiso-31-g113: Vidi is the passato remoto (I saw). Parte is 'a part/portion'; "ne lo stremo" = 'in the extremity/at the far edge' where ne = 'in/within' and lo stremo = 'the extreme/edge'. Vincer is an infinitive (poetic form) meaning 'to surpass/prevail over', used here in the sense '(was) victorious/overcame'. "Di lume" ('by/with light') expresses the means or quality by which it prevails, and "tutta l’altra fronte" ('the whole other front/face') is the thing being outshone. The clause thus says that part of the far rim, by its light, overpowered the entire opposite face with brightness.
segments.paradiso-31-g114: E come ... introduces a simile (“And as ...”). quivi ove s’aspetta is a relative/adverbial clause: quivi = 'there', ove = 'where', and s’aspetta is the impersonal/reflexive si + aspetta = 'is awaited' or 'one awaits'. il temo is the definite article + noun (here 'temo' is an archaic/poetic form meaning 'the time' or occasion awaited) and che introduces a subordinate clause explaining that time: che mal guidò Fetonte = 'that Phaethon guided badly' (guidò is simple past).
segments.paradiso-31-g115: più s’infiamma is a short predicate: più = 'more' and s’infiamma is the pronominal/intransitive verb infiammarsi = 'become inflamed' or 'flare up' (s’ = si reflexive/impersonal). The phrase means 'it flares up more.'
segments.paradiso-31-g116: e quinci e quindi = 'and from this side and from that' (quinci/quindi are locative adverbs in coordinate opposition). il lume si fa scemo = 'the light makes/becomes dim'; si fa is 'becomes' (si + fare used intransitively) and scemo here means 'diminished/dimmed' rather than 'foolish' (an adjectival predicate).
segments.paradiso-31-g117: così introduces the continuation of the simile ('thus/so'). quella pacifica oriafiamma is a noun phrase: quella = 'that', pacifica = 'peaceful', oriafiamma = a compound/poetic noun 'golden‑flame' (an epithet). nel mezzo s’avvivava is locative + verb: nel mezzo = 'in the middle' and s’avvivava (avvivarsi) is imperfect tense of a pronominal verb meaning 'became more alive/brightened' (s’ = si).
segments.paradiso-31-g118: e d’ogne parte per igual modo = 'and from every side in equal manner' (d’ = di elided, ogne = every, per igual modo = 'in the same way'). allentava la fiamma is an imperfect active verb allentare 'to slacken/loosen' with la fiamma as direct object: 'it slackened the flame.'
segments.paradiso-31-g119: e a quel mezzo = 'and at that middle/center' — a short locative phrase with a = 'at/to' + quel mezzo = 'that middle/center,' linking to what follows.
segments.paradiso-31-g120: con le penne sparte is an instrumental/participial phrase: con = 'with', le penne = 'the plumes/feathers' (angelic wings), sparte = 'spread' (here adjectival/participle meaning 'laid out/spread'). The whole phrase describes the angels' appearance.
segments.paradiso-31-g121: vid’ io più di mille angeli festanti is the main reporting clause: vid’ (apocopated vidi) = 'I saw', io = 'I' (emphatic), più di mille = 'more than a thousand', angeli = 'angels', festanti = present participle 'rejoicing/celebrating'.
segments.paradiso-31-g122: ciascun distinto di fulgore e d’arte modifies 'angels': ciascun = 'each one', distinto = 'distinguished/separate', di fulgore e d’arte = 'by/with splendor and skill' (di + abstract nouns). The phrase means each angel was notable by his brightness and skill.
segments.paradiso-31-g123: Vidi a lor giochi quivi e a lor canti sets the scene: Vidi = 'I saw', a lor giochi/canti = 'at their games/at their songs' (a + possessive lor = 'their'), quivi = 'there'. ridere una bellezza places the subject after the locatives: ridere = 'to laugh/beam' and una bellezza = 'a beauty' (subject).
segments.paradiso-31-g124: che letizia era ne li occhi a tutti li altri santi is a relative clause describing that beauty: che = 'which/that', letizia = 'joy', era = imperfect 'was', ne li occhi = 'in the eyes', a tutti li altri santi = 'of all the other saints' (a = 'to/of' in this idiom).
segments.paradiso-31-g125: e s’io avessi in dir tanta divizia quanta ad imaginar is a conditional concessive clause: e s’io avessi = 'and if I had' (s’ = si in subjunctive/apodosis construction), in dir = 'in saying/speech', tanta divizia = 'so great an abundance', quanta ad imaginar = 'as much as in imagining' (quanta = 'as much as').
segments.paradiso-31-g126: non ardirei lo minimo tentar di sua delizia is the apodosis: non ardirei = 'I would not dare' (conditional of ardire), lo minimo = 'the least/ smallest', tentar = infinitive 'to attempt', di sua delizia = 'of her delight' (possessive sua). The whole clause means he would not dare even the smallest attempt at describing her delight.
segments.paradiso-31-g127: Bernardo is simply the vocative/name introducing the following action; punctuation indicates the direct address.
segments.paradiso-31-g128: come vide li occhi miei nel caldo suo caler fissi e attenti: come = 'as/when', vide = past 'he saw', li occhi miei = 'my eyes', nel caldo suo = 'into/within his warmth' (caldo = warmth, suo = his), caler (poetic infinitive/archaic form of calare) = 'to sink/descend' here used with a visual effect, and fissi e attenti are predicative adjectives 'fixed and attentive' describing the eyes.
segments.paradiso-31-g129: li suoi con tanto affetto volse a lei = 'he turned his (eyes) to her with such affection': li suoi (elliptical for 'his eyes'), con tanto affetto = 'with so much affection', volse = past of volgere 'to turn/direct', a lei = 'to her'. The verb volse takes the indirect object a + pronoun.
segments.paradiso-31-g130: che ’ miei di rimirar fé più ardenti is a result clause introduced by che = 'so that/so which': ’ miei = 'my (eyes)' (apostrophe marks elision), di rimirar = 'from/of gazing' (rimirar = to gaze), fé = past of fare used causatively 'made', più ardenti = 'more ardent' (comparative adjective). So: 'which made mine more ardent in gazing.'
segments.paradiso-31-g15: Questo is a demonstrative adjective meaning "this" and directly modifies the masculine singular noun regno. The two adjectives sicuro and gaudïoso follow regno in apposition connected by e; sicuro here means "secure/certain" and gaudïoso (with a diaeresis marking syllable division in the poem) means "joyful." The whole phrase is a nominal phrase in nominative (subject/topic) position, set off by the comma for the next clause.
segments.paradiso-31-g16: Frequent e + prepositional phrases: frequente is an adjective meaning "frequent" or "well-attended" and the prepositional phrase in gente antica e in novella specifies among whom the realm is frequented. Gente is a collective noun (people), antica modifies it (ancient), and in novella repeats the preposition with novella (here used adjectivally to mean "new/recent" people). The whole phrase is an appositive/participial-like modifier of regno.
segments.paradiso-31-g17: Two nouns joined by e (viso e amore) form the grammatical subject of avea. Avea is the archaic imperfect form of avere (3rd singular) used as the main verb meaning "had" or "possessed." Tutto ad un segno is a prepositional-phrase complement: tutto = entirely/all, ad = "to/toward" (here indicating direction or focus), un segno = "one sign/mark". Together the clause says both sight/face and love were wholly directed toward one mark.
segments.paradiso-31-g18: O is the vocative/exclamatory particle "O." Trina here is an adjective meaning "threefold/triune" (addressing the Trinity) modifying luce (light). The relative clause che ’n unica stella scintillando a lor vista uses the participle scintillando (sparkling) with a contracted prepositional phrase '’n unica' (= in una unica, "in a single") modifying stella; a lor vista = "to their sight/ before their eyes." The participle supplies circumstantial detail about how that light appears to them.
segments.paradiso-31-g19: Sì is an adverb meaning "so/thus" (an emphatic result marker here). Li is the enclitic object pronoun "them" (referring back to the souls being described). Appaga is 3rd-person singular present of appagare, "to satisfy/please/gratify." The exclamation asserts that the threefold light, as it sparkles in that one star, so satisfies them.
segments.paradiso-31-g20: Guarda is the imperative (or exclamatory present) of guardare, meaning "look" or "see" addressed to the addressee. Qua giuso is an adverbial locution: qua = "here/hither," giuso = archaic for "down/below." A la nostra procella is a prepositional phrase: a + the definite article la (to/at the) + nostra procella (our storm/uproar). The whole is a direct imperative urging the addressee to look down at the speaker’s storm below.
segments.paradiso-31-g21: Se is the conditional/subordinating conjunction "if." I barbari is the definite plural subject "the barbarians." The clause opens a conditional/hypothetical protasis that continues through the following participial and subordinate clauses.
segments.paradiso-31-g22: Venendo is a gerund/participle of venire meaning "coming" and introduces a circumstantial clause ("coming from..."). Da tal plaga = "from such a region/land" (tal = such; plaga = region/district). The relative clause che ciascun giorno d’Elice si cuopra modifies plaga: che = "which," ciascun giorno = "each day," d’Elice = "by/with Helice," si cuopra is a reflexive/impersonal form of cuoprire (archaic for coprire, "cover"), meaning "is covered."
segments.paradiso-31-g23: Rotante is a present participle meaning "turning/rotating" and continues to describe that region (or the heavenly body associated with it). Col is the contracted form of con + il "with the," suo figlio = "its/her son/child." Ond' is a contracted form of onde or 'onda' meaning "from which/whence," and ella is the feminine pronoun referring back to the preceding feminine noun (the region or Helice). È vaga: è is the present of essere, and vaga is an adjective that here conveys the sense of being "fond/attracted" (poetic use).
segments.paradiso-31-g24: Veggendo is a present participle of the rare verb veggere, meaning "beholding/seeing," and here introduces what the barbarians see. Roma is the object; e l’ardüa sua opra = "and her arduous/hard work" (l’ = the, ardüa = "arduous" used poetically, sua opra = "her work/achievement"). The participial clause links the visual impression to their astonishment.
segments.paradiso-31-g25: Stupefaciensi is an archaic/deponent reflexive verb form meaning "they were astonished" or "they stupefied themselves." The -si ending marks the reflexive construction; grammatically it functions as the main verb of the clause referring back to the barbarians.
segments.paradiso-31-g26: Quando = "when." Laterano is the subject (the Lateran, i.e., the papal seat). A le cose mortali andò di sopra is an idiomatic clause: a le cose mortali = "over/beyond mortal things," and andò (passato remoto of andare) = "went/rose," di sopra = "above/on high." The line situates the moment of astonishment (when the Lateran rose above mortal affairs).
segments.paradiso-31-g27: ïo is the emphatic first-person pronoun "I" (the diaeresis marks the syllabic separation for the poem's meter). It begins the speaker's reply to the hypothetical astonishment just described.
segments.paradiso-31-g28: Che is a relative pronoun linking the following description to ïo. Al divino da l’umano is a pair of prepositional phrases: al (a + il) divino = "to the divine," da l’ (da + il) umano = "from the human." The order is rhetorical: "from the human to the divine."
segments.paradiso-31-g29: A l’etterno dal tempo era venuto continues the movement with a + l’ etterno = "to the eternal," dal (da + il) tempo = "from time." Era venuto is the compound past (auxiliary essere + past participle venuto) meaning "had come" or "had arrived." Grammatically this is a perfect/pluperfect sense describing the speaker's transition.
segments.paradiso-31-g30: E di Fiorenza in popol giusto e sano: e = "and." Di Fiorenza = "from Florence" (di used for origin), in popol = "into/among a people" (in + popol = among the populace), giusto e sano are coordinate adjectives modifying popol, meaning "righteous/just and sound/healthy." The phrase describes the social context the speaker left.
segments.paradiso-31-g31: Di che stupor is an exclamative prepositional phrase meaning "of what astonishment" or "what astonishment." Dovea is the imperfect of dovere used here to express obligation or expectation ("ought to/was bound to"). Esser compiuto uses the infinitive esser + past participle compiuto ("to be filled/completed") and forms the predicate: literally, "of what astonishment I ought to have been filled!" — an exclamation about the speaker's expected amazement.
segments.paradiso-31-g1: In forma dunque di candida rosa is a fronted prepositional phrase ('in the form of a white rose'). Mi si mostrava is a reflexive/impersonal construction with the indirect-object pronoun mi ('to me') and mostrava (imperfect indicative of mostrarsi) meaning 'was shown/appeared to me'; the grammatical subject (la milizia santa) follows the verb. The relative clause che nel suo sangue Cristo fece sposa uses the past historic fece (fare) meaning 'made' and sposa as object/complement: 'which Christ made bride in his blood.'
segments.paradiso-31-g2: Ma is the simple adversative 'but'. L’ (elided article) + altra = 'the other' (feminine). This short segment functions as the contrastive subject introduction for the following relative clause.
segments.paradiso-31-g3: This relative clause modifies 'l'altra'. Volando is a present participle/gerund ('flying') describing the subject; vede e canta are present indicative verbs ('sees and sings'). The two parallel objects ('la gloria' and 'la bontà') are expanded by further relative clauses: colui che la ’nnamora ('he who enamors it') and la bontà che la fece cotanta ('the goodness that made it so great'). Note the archaic contraction ’nnamora for innamora.
segments.paradiso-31-g4: Sì come introduces a simile ('just as'). Schiera d'ape is 'a host/swarm of bees'; che s’infiora is a relative clause where s’ is the reflexive pronoun and infiora is a verb meaning roughly 'alight on/visit flowers' (poetic usage). Una fïata 'one time/once' and una si ritorna 'and one returns' describe alternating motion; là dove suo laboro s’insapora is a locative clause 'to where its labor is tasted/enjoyed' with s’insapora a passive/reflexive form ('is flavored/enjoyed').
segments.paradiso-31-g5: Nel gran fior discendeva: discendeva is imperfect indicative ('was descending' or 'alighted') describing repeated/habitual motion into the large flower (gran fior). Che s’addorna di tante foglie is a relative clause where s’addorna is pronominal ('is adorned' / 'adorns itself') and di tante foglie states the adornment material ('with so many leaves').
segments.paradiso-31-g6: E quindi risaliva links the previous downward motion to the upward one: risaliva is imperfect ('rose again' / 'went back up'), and quindi here means 'then/from there'. Là dove ’l süo amor sempre soggiorna is a locative relative clause: lì/dove 'where' + the poetic possessive süo and soggiorna (present indicative) 'dwells/stays always.'
segments.paradiso-31-g7: Le facce tutte avean is an archaic imperfect form (avean = avevano) meaning 'their faces all had / were all of'. Di fiamma viva indicates material/quality ('of living flame'). E l’ali d’oro continues with 'and the wings of gold' (l’ali = le ali, article elided before vowel).
segments.paradiso-31-g8: E l’altro introduces a contrastive second element: 'and the other'. Tanto bianco uses tanto as an intensifier ('so/so very') modifying the adjective bianco ('white').
segments.paradiso-31-g9: Che introduces a result clause: 'so ... that'. Nulla neve a quel termine arriva means literally 'no snow reaches that limit/term' — arriva is present indicative 'reaches', and a quel termine is an idiomatic locution meaning 'to that degree/limit.'
segments.paradiso-31-g10: Quando scendean nel fior: Quando 'when'; scendean is an archaic imperfect plural (they were descending / alighting) — the -ean ending is an older inflectional form; nel fior = 'into the flower.'
segments.paradiso-31-g11: Di banco in banco is an idiom meaning 'from row/bench to row/bench' (movement along the petals). Porgevan is implied as 'they presented/offered' (archaic imperfect). De la pace e de l’ardore lists the things offered ('of the peace and of the ardor'). Ch’elli acquistavan is a relative clause with acquistavan (imperfect 'they were acquiring') and ventilando il fianco is a gerund phrase ('by fanning their flank/sides') indicating how they gained that ardor.
segments.paradiso-31-g12: Né is 'nor.' L’interporsi is a substantive use of the infinitive (the interposing/interposition); tra ’l disopra e ’l fiore = 'between the above/upper region and the flower.' Di tanta moltitudine volante qualifies that interposition ('of so great a flying multitude'). Impediva is the imperfect 'it impeded' and takes la vista e lo splendore as its objects.
segments.paradiso-31-g13: Ché introduces a causal explanation ('for/because'). La luce divina è penetrante uses the present verb è and the adjective/participle penetrante ('penetrating') describing the divine light. Per l’universo indicates the scope ('through the universe'), and secondo ch’è degno is a limitative phrase meaning 'according to what is worthy' (secondo = 'according to').
segments.paradiso-31-g14: Sì che = 'so that/therefore' introducing the consequence. Nulla le puote essere ostante: nulla 'nothing'; le is the dative/indirect pronoun referring back to 'la luce' (to it); puote is an archaic present of potere (can/is able) — here 'can'; essere ostante is an infinitive phrase 'to be opposing/standing in the way.' Altogether: 'nothing can be an obstacle to it.'
segments.paradiso-31-g45: Uno is an indefinite pronoun here meaning 'one (person)' as the subject. intendëa is the imperfect indicative (3rd singular) of intendere; the diaeresis on the e (intendëa) marks the vowel separation for the poem's meter and the verb here means 'to attend/listen'.
segments.paradiso-31-g46: e is the simple coordinating conjunction 'and'. altro is an indefinite subject meaning 'another (person)'. mi is the clitic indirect-object pronoun 'to me'. rispuose is passato remoto (3rd singular) of rispondere meaning 'answered/replied'.
segments.paradiso-31-g47: This is direct speech quoted after the colon. credea is the imperfect 1st singular of credere ('I thought' / 'I believed'). veder is the infinitive (poetic clipped form of vedere) used as the object of credea ('thought I saw'). vidi is passato remoto 1st singular of vedere ('I saw'). un + noun is the indefinite article; vestito is a past participle used adjectivally ('clothed') modifying sene; con le genti glorïose is a prepositional phrase 'with the glorious company/people'.
segments.paradiso-31-g48: Diffuso is a past participle/adjective fronted to form the predicate 'diffused' (literally 'spread out'); era is the imperfect/indicative 'was'. per li occhi e per le gene is a pair of prepositional phrases 'through his eyes and through his cheeks'. di benigna letizia is a genitive/qualification 'of benign joy' specifying what was spread.
segments.paradiso-31-g49: in atto pio is 'in a pious/compassionate gesture/attitude' (in + noun phrase). quale introduces a comparative/relative clause 'such as' and a + tenero padre si convene is a reflexive impersonal construction: convene (from convenire) in this sense means 'is fitting' or 'suits' — si convene = 'is fitting' and a tenero padre indicates to whom it is fitting.
segments.paradiso-31-g50: E is the conjunction 'and' linking this question to the preceding speech. Ov’ is an elided/poetic form of ove/ov'è ('where'), with the apostrophe marking the omitted vowel. è is the present indicative 3rd singular of essere ('is'). ella is the feminine subject pronoun 'she' (used here for emphasis/clarity).
segments.paradiso-31-g51: This segment is punctuation (a comma) marking the continuation from the reported question into the narrator's immediate reply; it contains no lexical items.
segments.paradiso-31-g52: sùbito is an adverb 'immediately/suddenly' placed before the verb for emphasis. diss' is the elided poetic form of dissi (passato remoto 1st singular of dire) with the final vowel dropped for meter; io is the subject pronoun 'I' placed after the verb (inversion typical of poetry) and reinforces the first person subject.
segments.paradiso-31-g69: Tu is the explicit subject 'you'. m' is the clitic object (mi = 'me') placed before the auxiliary hai. Hai ... tratto is a compound perfect (avere + past participle tratto from trarre) meaning 'have drawn/rescued'. Di servo expresses the state being left ('from being a servant') and a libertate uses the preposition a + noun (archaic libertate = libertà) to indicate the goal 'into freedom'. Per tutte quelle vie is a prepositional phrase 'by all those ways/means' modifying the verb phrase.
segments.paradiso-31-g70: Per tutt' i modi = 'by all the methods/means' (tutt' is the archaic/poetic form of tutti before a vowel). Che introduces a relative clause modifying modi. Di ciò fare literally 'of/to do this' (di + pronoun + infinitive) is an older idiom meaning 'to do this'. Avei is an archaic 2nd-person form of avere used here to indicate past possession of power ('you had'); la potestate = 'the authority/power' (archaic spelling).
segments.paradiso-31-g71: La tua magnificenza is a noun phrase 'your magnificence' (La + possessive tua + noun). In me = 'in me/within me'. Custodi is an imperative/hortative form ('guard/keep') addressed to the addressee (the verb appears as a simple command/request in Dante's register).
segments.paradiso-31-g72: Sì che introduces a result/purpose clause 'so that'. L’anima mia = the noun phrase 'my soul' (l' = elided definite article + anima + possessive mia). This clause is linked to the preceding imperative as its intended result.
segments.paradiso-31-g73: Che is the relative pronoun 'which' referring to l'anima. Fatt' is the elided past participle fatta (from fare) meaning 'made' and is placed before the auxiliary hai (you have), an archaic but common word order in poetry. Hai is the auxiliary 'you have' and sana (agreeing in gender with anima) means 'healthy/whole'. Together: 'which you have made whole.'
segments.paradiso-31-g74: Piacente a te is an adjective phrase 'pleasing to you' (piacente = pleasing). Dal corpo = 'from the body' (da + il → dal). Si disnodi is a reflexive construction (si + verb disnodare) meaning 'be untied/be freed'; here it has the force of a jussive/result clause ('may be untied' / 'be freed'). Word order places the source dal corpo before the reflexive verb, a normal poetic order.
segments.paradiso-31-g75: Così = 'thus/so' and orai is the 1st-person past tense of orare ('I prayed') — a simple past used to report the speaker's action. The short clause functions as the narrator's summary of his petition.
segments.paradiso-31-g76: E = 'and' connecting clauses. Quella is a demonstrative pronoun 'that one' used here to refer back to the lady (effectively 'she'); it is the subject of the verbs that follow.
segments.paradiso-31-g77: Sì lontana come parea = 'as distant as she appeared'. Sì functions as an intensifier 'so/that', lontana 'distant' modifies the subject, come 'as', and parea is the imperfect of parere ('she seemed/appeared'), giving a descriptive background.
segments.paradiso-31-g78: Sorrise is the passato remoto (3rd sing.) 'she smiled'. E = 'and'. Riguardommi is an archaic, enclitic form meaning 'she looked at / regarded me' (verb + clitic mi attached); it reports a single completed action. The clitic -mi marks the first-person object 'me'.
segments.paradiso-31-g79: Poi = 'then/afterwards'. Si tornò is the reflexive passato remoto (3rd sing.) 'she returned' (literally 'turned herself back'). A l'etterna fontana = 'to the eternal fountain' (a + l' = to the), with etterna agreeing in gender with fontana.
segments.paradiso-31-g80: E is the simple conjunction 'and'. 'l santo is the noun phrase 'the holy one' (the saint/holy man). sene here is the fused form se ne (se + ne) meaning 'he withdrew/removed himself' or 'went aside' — Dante often uses such fused adverbial forms; the colon marks the reported speech that follows.
segments.paradiso-31-g81: Acciò che introduces a purpose clause 'so that' plus a subjunctive: tu assommi perfettamente — assommi is present subjunctive (2nd/3rd person singular form used after acciò che) meaning 'you may attain/reach' and perfettamente is the adverb 'perfectly'. The clause expresses intent or purpose.
segments.paradiso-31-g82: disse is the simple past 'he said' (passato remoto in Dante) and marks the reporting verb for the speech just quoted.
segments.paradiso-31-g83: il tuo cammino is the direct address/topic 'your path/journey' (il + possessive + noun); the phrase stands as the object/subject of what follows in the reported speech.
segments.paradiso-31-g84: a che here means 'to which' (introducing the clause explaining the purpose or destination of Dante's journey). priego e amor santo are the agents 'prayer and holy love' (priego = entreaty/prayer, amor santo = holy love), and mandommi is a perfect/preterite form from mandare used impersonally 'sent me' or 'have sent me' (Dantean morphology where -mmi marks 'me').
segments.paradiso-31-g85: vola is an imperative ('fly' or 'let fly'), here applied metaphorically to the eyes: 'let your eyes fly'. con li occhi = 'with the eyes', per questo giardino = 'through this garden' (per + accusative indicating movement through). This is a vivid imperative telling Dante to scan the scene with his eyes.
segments.paradiso-31-g86: ché introduces the reason 'for/because'. veder lui is an infinitive clause 'to see it/him' (lui used here as an object pronoun referring back to the garden or sight); t’acconcerà is future 'will make/fit you' (acconcerà from acconciare 'to prepare/fit') with clitic t' = 'you'. lo sguardo 'the gaze' is the object that will be fitted 'più al montar' = 'more for mounting' (i.e., better prepared for the upward ascent) and per lo raggio divino specifies the means 'by the divine ray'.
segments.paradiso-31-g87: Simple nominal phrase: conjunction E ('and') begins the clause, followed by the definite article + noun phrase 'la regina del cielo' (the Queen of Heaven). 'del' is the contracted preposition 'di + il' marking possession/relationship ('queen of the heaven'). This phrase names the subject of the following verb.
segments.paradiso-31-g88: Relative/adverbial clause introduced by the archaic/poetic 'ond’' (short for 'onde' or 'da cui') meaning 'from whom' or 'whence'. 'ïo' is an emphatic form of 'io' (I) and 'ardo' is present indicative 'I burn' used figuratively; word order mirrors Latin-derived emphasis (ond' ïo ardo = 'from whom I burn'). 'tutto d’amor' is an adverbial/participle phrase meaning 'completely with love', where 'tutto' intensifies the state.
segments.paradiso-31-g89: 'ne farà' is a future tense (fare, 3rd person singular futuro) where clitic 'ne' refers back to some antecedent (here: for/through her or of that matter) and functions adverbially/pronominally; 'farà ogne grazia' means 'will bring/make every grace' (= 'will grant every favor'). 'ogne grazia' is an object noun phrase with the archaic form 'ogne' for 'ogni' ('every').
segments.paradiso-31-g90: 'però ch’' here functions as a causal connector (roughly 'because'/'for that'), introducing the explanation for the previous assertion. 'i’ sono' (archaic placement of the subject pronoun) is present indicative 'I am'; 'il suo fedel Bernardo' is a predicate noun phrase ('her faithful Bernard'), where 'fedel' is an older spelling of 'fedele'. The whole clause gives the reason justifying the promise of grace.
segments.paradiso-31-g91: Main interrogative clause using 'Qual è colui che...' (literally 'Which is that one who...'), functioning as 'Who is the man who...'. 'forse' (perhaps) is parenthetical; 'di Croazia viene' uses 'di' + place to mean 'from Croatia' and 'viene' is present indicative 'comes'. 'a veder la Veronica nostra' uses the infinitive 'veder' governed by 'a' to express purpose ('to see our Veronica').
segments.paradiso-31-g92: Relative clause 'che per l’antica fame non sen sazia' modifies the man. 'per l’antica fame' gives the motive/condition ('through/because of an old/longstanding hunger/craving'); 'non ... sazia' is present indicative negative 'is not satisfied'. 'sen' here is a fused form of 'se n'' (si + ne) or dialectal clitic usage pointing to the object, but the clause functions simply as 'is not sated'.
segments.paradiso-31-g93: Coordinating conjunction 'ma' ('but') introduces a contrast. 'dice' is present indicative 'he says' and 'nel pensier' ('in his thought') marks that this is an internal, mental utterance (reported thought). Word order is standard: verb + locative phrase.
segments.paradiso-31-g94: 'fin che' is a subordinating conjunction meaning 'until'; 'si mostra' is present indicative (reflexive/impersonal 'it shows itself' / 'it appears'). The subordinate sets the temporal limit for the mental utterance—he keeps thinking it 'until it shows itself' (until the image is revealed).
segments.paradiso-31-g95: Direct speech begins with the vocative/exclamation 'Segnor mio Iesù Cristo' ('My Lord Jesus Christ'). 'Segnor' is an archaic spelling of 'Signor' used as an address; word order places the title before the possessive 'mio' following poetic usage.
segments.paradiso-31-g96: Appositive epithet 'Dio verace' ('true God') placed after the vocative to stress the deity and truthfulness/genuineness of God; 'verace' is an old/poetic adjective meaning 'true' or 'genuine'.
segments.paradiso-31-g97: Interrogative sentence: 'or fu sì fatta la sembianza vostra?' uses passato remoto 'fu' ('was'), so this is a direct question in the past about the making of the likeness. 'or' = 'now/then' (here introducing the question), 'sì fatta' = 'made thus', and 'la sembianza vostra' = 'your likeness' (formal/poetic order 'your' after the noun).
segments.paradiso-31-g98: A short punctuation segment: the single apostrophe and semicolon mark the end of the preceding quoted thought or a pause in the narrative. It contains no lexical items to analyze.
segments.paradiso-31-g99: 'Tal era io' is a verbless/nominal phrase meaning 'such was I' or 'I was such', with 'tal' (such) modifying the state of the speaker. 'mirando' is a present participle 'gazing' that links to the main participial clause 'la vivace carità di colui...' The phrase describes the narrator's posture/attitude while observing the 'vivace carità' (lively/active charity) of the person mentioned.
segments.paradiso-31-g100: 'contemplando' is a present participle ('contemplating') that functions adverbially to describe how the man acted in life; it is terse poetry: participle alone standing in apposition to the subject in the previous phrase.
segments.paradiso-31-g101: 'gustò' is passato remoto (gustare, 3rd sing.) meaning 'he tasted' or figuratively 'he experienced/savored'; 'di quella pace' ('of that peace') is the object of gustò. The construction 'gustò di' is poetic for 'partook of' or 'tasted that peace'.
segments.paradiso-31-g102: Vocative phrase 'Figliuol di grazia' ('Son of grace') addressing the listener; 'Figliuol' (variant spelling of 'figlio') + prepositional phrase 'di grazia' is an honorific vocative used by a speaker to address someone respectfully.
segments.paradiso-31-g103:
'quest' esser giocondo' is a nominal phrase; 'quest
esser' is an archaic/poetic order for 'questo essere' ('this being/existence'), and 'giocondo' should mean 'joyful' or 'delightful' (an adjective after the noun). The snippet is a compressed epithet describing the state to be contemplated.
segments.paradiso-31-g104: 'cominciò elli' is simple past narration: 'cominciò' (pas. rem. 'he began') with 'elli' (archaic third-person pronoun) as the subject; word order verb + subject is common in narrative to mark a new utterance.
segments.paradiso-31-g105: Negative clause 'non ti sarà noto' uses future passive/impersonal sense 'will not be known to you' ('ti' = to you). 'noto' is adjective 'known' and 'sarà' is future of essere, forming the future passive sense 'will be known'.
segments.paradiso-31-g106: Adverbial participial and prepositional phrase describing the condition: 'tenendo li occhi pur qua giù al fondo' literally 'keeping the eyes still here down to the bottom' (present participle 'tenendo' = 'if you keep/holding'); 'li occhi' is direct object, and 'qua giù al fondo' marks the location/direction to keep them.
segments.paradiso-31-g107: Imperative/paratactic instruction: 'ma guarda i cerchi infino al più remoto' — 'ma' ('but') contrasts the previous clause; 'guarda' is the imperative 'look' (2nd person sg.), 'i cerchi' literally 'the circles' (here: the concentric spheres/levels of Paradise), 'infino al più remoto' = 'as far as the most remote'.
segments.paradiso-31-g108: 'tanto che veggi seder la regina' is a temporal/result clause 'until you see the queen seated' where 'tanto che' = 'until/so that', 'veggi' is present subjunctive/archaic form of 'vedere' used imperatively/optatively here ('that you may see'), and 'seder' (infinitive) means 'to sit' or 'be seated'. The relative clause 'cui questo regno è suddito e devoto' uses 'cui' ('to whom'/'of whom') to indicate the queen is the one to whom the kingdom is subject and loyal; note that 'suddito' here in meaning 'subject' is expected, but the supplied sense list contained unrelated glosses, so that sense is understood from context as 'subject'.

sentences.paradiso-31-s9: Certainly, between that and the joy it made me inclined not to hear and to remain silent.
sentences.paradiso-31-s10: And like a pilgrim who refreshes himself by looking back in the temple of his vow, and already hopes to say again how he had been — walking up through the living light — I moved my eyes along the steps, now up, now down, now circling again.
sentences.paradiso-31-s11: I saw faces moved by charity, adorned by another’s light and by her smile, and gestures adorned with every kind of honesty.
sentences.paradiso-31-s12: The general form of Paradise my whole gaze had already encompassed, fixed on no single part as yet; and I turned, with rekindled desire, to ask my lady about the things on which my mind was suspended.
sentences.paradiso-31-s17: Thereupon he said: "Beatrice moved me from my place to put an end to your longing; and if you look up to the third circle from the highest degree, you will see her again on the throne that her merits have assigned her."
sentences.paradiso-31-s18: Without answering, I lifted up my eyes, and I saw her who was making a crown, reflecting the eternal rays from herself.
sentences.paradiso-31-s19: From that region which sounds highest above no mortal eye is so far removed — however much any (eye) that plunges lower into the sea — as my sight was from Beatrice there; but it did nothing to me, for her likeness did not descend to me through a mixed medium.
sentences.paradiso-31-s20: “O lady in whom my hope lives, and who endured, for my salvation, to leave your traces in Hell, from so many things as I have seen I recognize, from your power and from your goodness, grace and virtue.”
sentences.paradiso-31-s29: I lifted my eyes; and just as at morning the eastern part of the horizon outshines that part where the sun sinks, so, as if with my eyes going from the valley up toward the mountain, I saw a portion at the farthest rim, by means of light, overcome the whole opposite face.
sentences.paradiso-31-s30: And as there, where one awaits the time that Phaethon ill‑guided, it flames up more, and from this side and from that the light grows dim, so that peaceful golden‑flame in the middle brightened, and on every side in equal fashion it slackened the flame; and at that middle, with the plumes spread, I saw more than a thousand rejoicing angels, each distinguished by splendor and skill.
sentences.paradiso-31-s31: I saw, at their plays there and at their songs, a beauty laughing, which joy was in the eyes of all the other saints; and if I had in speaking so much abundance as in imagining, I would not dare to attempt the smallest thing of her delight.
sentences.paradiso-31-s32: Bernard, when he saw my eyes fixed and intent sink into his warmth, turned his own toward her with such affection that mine were made more ardent in gazing.
sentences.paradiso-31-s5: This secure and joyous realm, frequented by people both ancient and recent, had sight and love entirely directed to one sign.
sentences.paradiso-31-s6: O threefold light, which, sparkling in a single star to their sight, so satisfies them!
sentences.paradiso-31-s7: Look here downward at our storm!
sentences.paradiso-31-s8: If the barbarians, coming from such a region that Helice covers every day, turning with her son of whom she is fond, when they beheld Rome and her arduous work were astonished — when the Lateran went above mortal things — I, who had come from the human to the divine, from time to the eternal, and from Florence among a just and wholesome people, of what astonishment ought I to have been filled!
sentences.paradiso-31-s1: So the holy host that Christ made his bride with his blood appeared to me in the form of a white rose; but the other — which, flying, sees and sings the glory of him who enamors it and the goodness that made it so great — like a swarm of bees that alights on a flower and then returns to where its labor is enjoyed, descended into the great blossom that is adorned with so many leaves, and then rose again to where its love forever dwells.
sentences.paradiso-31-s2: All their faces were of living flame and their wings of gold, and the other so white that no snow reaches that limit.
sentences.paradiso-31-s3: When they descended into the flower, from bench to bench they offered the peace and the ardor that they were acquiring by fanning their sides.
sentences.paradiso-31-s4: Nor did the interposition between the upper region and the flower of so great a flying multitude impede the sight or the splendour; for the divine light is penetrating through the universe according to what is worthy, so that nothing can stand in its way.
sentences.paradiso-31-s13: One person listened, and another answered me: "I thought I saw Beatrice, and I saw an elder clothed with the glorious company."
sentences.paradiso-31-s14: Benign joy was spread through his eyes and across his cheeks, in a pious expression such as becomes a tender father.
sentences.paradiso-31-s21: You have drawn me out of servitude into freedom by all those ways, by every means that you had the power to use to do this.
sentences.paradiso-31-s22: Keep your magnificence within me, so that my soul, which you have made whole, may, pleasing to you, be untied from the body.
sentences.paradiso-31-s23: Thus I prayed; and she, as distant as she seemed, smiled and looked at me; then she returned to the eternal fountain.
sentences.paradiso-31-s24: And the holy one withdrew; 'So that you may reach your journey perfectly,' he said, 'to which prayer and holy love have sent me, fly with your eyes through this garden; for seeing it will better fit your gaze for mounting by the divine ray.'
sentences.paradiso-31-s25: And the Queen of Heaven, from whom I burn completely with love, will grant every grace for that, because I am her faithful Bernard.
sentences.paradiso-31-s26: Who is the man who perhaps comes from Croatia to see our Veronica, who, because of his ancient hunger, is not satisfied with it, but says in his thought, until it appears: 'My Lord Jesus Christ, true God, was your likeness made thus?'
sentences.paradiso-31-s27: '; such was I, gazing at the living charity of the one who in this world, contemplating, tasted that peace.
sentences.paradiso-31-s28: "Son of grace, this joyful being," he began, "will not be known to you if you keep your eyes down here at the bottom; but look at the circles up to the most remote, until you see the queen sit, to whom this kingdom is subject and devoted."
